text: The surgeon identifies a tumor in the transverse colon and proceeds with a transverse colectomy. The transverse colon is suspended using a hanging maneuver on both the left and right sides. The surgical field reveals the pancreas as the surgeon enters the lesser sac. Dissection begins from the root of the mesentery to remove the lymphovascular tissue along with the tumor. The procedure is performed using energy devices without sutures, successfully excising the lymphovascular pedicle from below and behind the pancreas.

The surgeon opens the lesser sac and skeletonizes the left-sided colon. Margins are taken on both sides of the transverse colon, and a stapler is fired on the left and right to perform the transverse colectomy. The surgeon mobilizes the colon further, removing omentum from both sides to achieve a tension-free colocolic anastomosis. The hepatic flexure is dismantled, and the right colon is mobilized two-thirds from above downwards.

The surgeon gradually dissects and mobilizes the duodenum and hepatic flexure, working in a plane anterior to Gerota's fascia. The right colon is properly taken down, followed by attention to the left side where the colon is separated from the omentum. The splenic flexure is dismantled, with dissection proceeding from medial to lateral, mobilizing the proximal one-third of the left colon. These steps are necessary due to the complete removal of the transverse colon, requiring both the right and left flexures to be brought down.

The surgeon performs an isoperistaltic side-to-side colocolostomy using a blue cartridge from the patient's left side port. The staple line is reinforced with a crotch stitch to alleviate tension, followed by additional stitches to ensure the staple hole remains unobstructed. An extramucosal continuous suturing technique is applied, and the specimen is extracted from a fan instilling position.
